steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what both authors would most likely agree on. Text 1 says Meding and Zasoski used barriers to keep root systems separate, blocking root-to-root transfer. Text 2 also acknowledges those barriers separated the root systems, thereby excluding root-to-root transmission — even while criticizing the study on other grounds. The point of agreement is that the barriers did exclude root-to-root transfer.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The barriers used in Meding and Zasoski's study effectively excluded root-to-root transmission of nutrients.': Both texts confirm this: Text 1 describes it as a "crucial step" and Text 2 explicitly grants that the barriers separated root systems. ✓
  • Choice B — 'A barrier that is impervious to both roots and fungal strands is necessary to evaluate nutrient transfer via a CMN.': Text 2 suggests that liquid permeability is the remaining problem, not fungal-strand permeability, and Text 1 never raises this requirement. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Excluding root-to-root transfer is sufficient to ensure any observed transfer must involve a CMN.': Text 2 explicitly rejects this — it argues the permeable barrier could also allow liquid transfer. ✗
  • Choice D — 'He and colleagues' study did not find convincing evidence of nutrient transfer between individual plants.': Neither text makes this claim about He and colleagues' results. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
